## SECTION 7 — This Day in History (World)
On February 4, 1945, the massive, highly critical Yalta Conference officially began in the massive Livadia Palace in Crimea. The "Big Three" Allied leaders—US President Franklin D. Roosevelt, British Prime Minister Winston Churchill, and Soviet Premier Joseph Stalin—convened to heavily negotiate the massive post-war reorganization of Germany and Europe. The massive, highly controversial agreements reached at Yalta effectively divided Europe into massive, heavily entrenched Western and Soviet spheres of influence, fundamentally setting the immediate stage for the massive, decades-long Cold War.

On February 4, 1948, the nation of Sri Lanka, then officially known as Ceylon, formally achieved massive, absolute independence from the British Empire. Following decades of massive, largely peaceful political campaigning for self-rule, the island nation transitioned into an independent dominion within the British Commonwealth. The massive, highly celebrated event marked the beginning of self-governance, but deeply embedded ethnic tensions between the Sinhalese majority and the Tamil minority would eventually aggressively plunge the nation into a massive, devastating decades-long civil war.

## SECTION 8 — This Day in History (India)
On February 4, 1922, the massive, highly tragic Chauri Chaura incident occurred during the massive Non-Cooperation Movement. A large group of massive, peaceful protesters in the United Provinces (Uttar Pradesh) clashed heavily with the local police, who opened fire. In aggressive, massive retaliation, the enraged mob heavily attacked and set fire to the local police station, resulting in the massive, horrific deaths of 22 policemen. This massive violence deeply horrified Mahatma Gandhi, who immediately and aggressively halted the entire, massive national movement.

On February 4, 1974, Satyendra Nath Bose, the massive, brilliant Indian physicist and mathematician, passed away in Calcutta. Best known for his massive, foundational collaboration with Albert Einstein in the 1920s, he heavily developed the massive foundation for Bose-Einstein statistics and the massive theory of the Bose-Einstein condensate. The massive, fundamental class of subatomic particles that obey his statistics, the "bosons" (which includes the massive Higgs boson), was officially named in his absolute, massive honor, cementing his legacy in global quantum mechanics.
